And if the governance and disposal belong to the Exalted, then He is the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين) and the God (الإله) who is worshipped and thanked for His blessings and kindness.

This is what His saying points to: لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْغَنِيُ "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Indeed Allah is the All-sufficient." Thus, His saying: لِلَّهِ ما فِي "To Allah belongs..." etc., is a proof for His oneness, and His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْغَنِيُ "Indeed Allah is the All-sufficient" is the reason (تعليل) for the ownership.

As for His saying: الْحَمِيدُ "the All-laudable," meaning the one praised in His acts, it is another principle for the proof. This is because praise (الحمد) is the commendation of a beautiful voluntary act, and every beauty in the world belongs to Him, the Glorified; thus, commendation for it returns to Him. He is therefore All-laudable (حميد) in an absolute sense. If any part of this precise and beautiful governance were from other than the Exalted, without attribution to Him, then the praise and commendation would belong to that other and not to Him, so He would not be All-laudable in an absolute sense and in relation to everything—and it has been posited that He is All-laudable in an absolute sense; this is a contradiction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ أَقْلامٌ وَالْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ ما نَفِدَتْ كَلِماتُ اللهِ "If all the trees on the earth were pens, and the sea [replenished] by seven more seas [were ink], the words of Allah would not be spent..." (31:27) etc. مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ "of trees" is an explanation for the relative pronoun (الموصول), and "tree" (الشجرة) is the singular of "trees" (الشجر) and signifies in this context—being in the context of "if" (لَوْ)—exhaustiveness (الاستغراق); i.e., every tree on earth. What is meant by "the sea" (البحر) is the sea in an absolute sense. And His saying: يَمُدُّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ "replenished by seven more seas" means seven like it assist it by joining it. It is apparent that "seven" is intended to denote multiplicity rather than this specific number. The word (الكلمة) is the utterance indicating a meaning, and in His speech, the Exalted, it has been applied to the existence bestowed by His command. He has said: إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is." (36:82). The term "word" (الكلمة) was also applied to the Messiah (peace be upon him) in His saying: وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى مَرْيَمَ "and His Word that He cast toward Mary" (4:171).

The meaning is: if the trees of the earth were made into pens, and the sea were taken and seven like it were added to it and the whole were made into ink, and the words of Allah were written—by transforming them into utterances indicating them—with those pens from that ink, the sea would be exhausted before the words of Allah were exhausted, because they are infinite.

From here it becomes clear that there is a conciseness through omission (إيجاز بالحذف) in the speech, and that His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ "Indeed Allah is All-mighty, All-wise" is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل). The meaning is: because He, the Exalted, is All-mighty (عزيز), nothing can overcome or subjugate Him, so this writing does not exhaust what is from Him; and He is All-wise (حكيم), He does not delegate governance to another.

The verse is connected to what preceded it in terms of its indication that the governance of creation belongs to Him, the Glorified, and not to another. Thus, this verse was brought to indicate the vastness of His governance and the multiplicity of His creative commands (أوامره التكوينية) in creation and governance to the point where the sea replenished by seven like it would be exhausted if it were made into ink and the trees of the earth made into pens were used to write with it, before His commands and words were exhausted.

His saying, the Exalted: ما خَلْقُكُمْ وَلا بَعْثُكُمْ إِلَّا كَنَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ إِنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ "Your creation and your resurrection are not but as of a single soul. Indeed Allah is All-hearing, All-seeing." (31:28). This directs the speech toward the possibility of the Gathering (الحشر), especially in light of their deeming the Return (المعاد) far-fetched due to the large number of the dead and their mixing with the earth without distinction of one from another.

So the Exalted said: ما خَلْقُكُمْ وَلا بَعْثُكُمْ إِلَّا كَنَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ "Your creation and your resurrection are not but as of a single soul" in terms of possibility and feasibility. For He, the Exalted, is not distracted by one affair from another, nor does multiplicity frustrate Him, and the one and the many are the same in relation to Him. He mentioned creation along with resurrection to indicate the lack of difference between the beginning and the return in terms of ease and difficulty; rather, His act is not described by ease or difficulty.

What has been mentioned is evidenced by the addition of creation and resurrection to the plural second-person pronoun, referring to the people, and then its comparison to a single soul. The meaning is: the creation of you, O people, despite your multiplicity, and your resurrection, is nothing but like the creation of a single soul and its resurrection. You, in your multiplicity, and the single soul are equal, because if the resurrection of all despite their multiplicity and the resurrection for the recompense of deeds were difficult, it would only be difficult from the aspect of ignorance of your various deeds despite their multiplicity and their being mixed with one another; but He is not ignorant of any of them, because He is All-hearing of your words and All-seeing of your deeds. In other words, He is All-knowing of your deeds by way of witnessing.

By what has passed, the objection to the verse is refuted—namely, that it would be more appropriate to justify the creation and resurrection of the many being like a single soul with something like our saying: "Indeed Allah is powerful over all things" or "All-mighty, All-powerful" or what resembles that, rather than "All-hearing, All-seeing," which has no connection to creation and resurrection.

This is because the difficulty that the verse set out to repel is that resurrection is for the recompense of deeds, and given their multiplicity and their being merged into one another, how can they be distinguished so as to be recompensed? Thus, the difficulty is directed toward what He mentioned three verses prior in His saying: فَنُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا "then We will inform them of what they did." The answer was: how could any of the words and deeds be hidden from Him when He is All-hearing and All-seeing, such that no word or act escapes His witnessing?

The end of His previous saying: فَنُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا "then We will inform them of what they did" was His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ "Indeed Allah knows best what is in the breasts." This is based on the fact that recompense is according to what the heart carries of good and evil, as indicated by His saying: وَإِنْ تُبْدُوا ما فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ "Whether you reveal what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it" (2:284). This is an answer to this difficulty if it were directed at what the hearts carry despite their multiplicity; it is answered that Allah is All-knowing of what is in the breasts. And if it were directed at the external deeds themselves, consisting of words and acts, then the answer to it is what is in this verse we are considering: إِنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ "Indeed Allah is All-hearing, All-seeing." Thus, the difficulty and the answer are, in a way, similar to what occurred in His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَما بالُ الْقُرُونِ الْأُولى قالَ عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي فِي كِتابٍ لا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلا يَنْسى "He said, ‘What about the former generations?’ He said, ‘Their knowledge is with my Lord in a Book. My Lord neither errs nor forgets.’" (20:51-52). So understand this.

Others have answered the objection with other incomplete answers; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the lengthy commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَيُولِجُ النَّهارَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَسَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ يَجْرِي إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى "Have you not seen that Allah makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He has disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term..." (31:29) etc. This is an attestation to what preceded in the previous verse regarding His knowledge of deeds, through the governance (التدبير) occurring in the system of night and day, where this one increases and that one decreases and vice versa according to the different seasons and the various regions of the earth in a stable system that proceeds despite its variation. Likewise, the governance occurring in the sun and the moon in terms of the variation of their rising and setting and the variation of their course and path according to the senses, and each of them moves for a specified term, and there is no variation or confusion in the precise system they possess. All of this is impossible without knowledge and expertise from their Governor.

What is meant by making the night pass into the day is the night taking from the length and occupying some of the hours of the day from before, and by making the day pass into the night, the opposite of that. What is meant by the movement of the disposed sun and moon to a specified term is the ending of each of their states at a limited, predetermined time, then their return to the beginning. Whoever witnesses this precise, ongoing system and contemplates it will not doubt that its Governor only governs it out of knowledge unmixed with ignorance, and that it is not by chance or coincidence.

And His saying: وَأَنَّ اللهَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ "and that Allah is well aware of what you do" is conjunctive to the position of أَنَّ اللهَ يُولِجُ "that Allah makes... pass." The estimation is: "Have you not seen that Allah is well aware of what you do?" This is because whoever witnesses the system of the night and day and the sun and the moon would hardly be heedless of the fact that its Maker is All-knowing of the grand and minute aspects of his deeds; so it has been said.

However, there is a point here: that the deduction of knowledge of deeds from knowledge of the system occurring in the night and day and the sun and the moon, while correct in itself, is an inferential knowledge (علم حدسي) that does not justify calling it "seeing" (رؤية), and this is apparent.

Perhaps what is meant by witnessing His expertise (خبرته) regarding deeds is that if man were to contemplate the system occurring in the deeds of his own soul—insofar as they issue from the human world—distributed on one hand into the deeds issuing from the outward faculties of hearing, sight, smell, taste, and touch, and those issuing from the inward faculties, whether perceptive or active; or on one hand into some of the faculties and tools or all of them; and on one hand into the attractive and repulsive; and on one hand into the years of life from childhood, adolescence, youth, and old age, and so on.

Then, in the interconnectedness of some with others, the employment of some for others, the soul's guidance to placing each in its appropriate position, and its movement with this caravan of faculties and deeds toward its goal of perfection and its happiness in the end, and its involvement in the pitfalls of the material world and the abode of adornment and trial—some being saved and some perishing.

If he contemplates this system that bewilders the minds, he will not doubt that it is a predetermination (تقدير) determined by his Lord and a system organized by his Maker, the All-knowing, All-powerful. Witnessing this wondrous scientific system is witnessing that "He is well aware of what they do." And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ ما يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الْباطِلُ وَأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ "That is because Allah is the Real, and whatever they invoke besides Him is the False, and because Allah is the All-exalted, the All-great." (31:30). Since the Glorified mentioned that from Him is the beginning of everything, so it relies upon Him in its existence and the governance of its affair, and that to Him is the return of everything without difference between the one and the many, and that those whom they invoke besides Him have no part in creation or command, He gathered all of this under one comprehensive explanation, saying, pointing to what preceded: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ ما يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الْباطِلُ "That is because Allah is the Real, and whatever they invoke besides Him is the False..." etc.

To clarify: the Real (الحق) is the established (الثابت) from the aspect of its establishment, and the False (الباطل) is the opposite of the Real, so it is the non-established from the aspect of its lack of establishment. His saying: بِأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُ "because Allah is the Real," with its inclusion of the separating pronoun (ضمير الفصل) and the definition of the predicate with the definite article (اللام), signifies restriction (القصر); i.e., restricting the subject to the predicate.

Thus, His saying: بِأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُ "because Allah is the Real" is a restriction of Him, the Exalted, to establishment; i.e., He is established such that no falsehood mixes with His establishment. In other words, He is established from all aspects. In a third way, He is existent under every estimation; thus His existence is absolute, not restricted by a restriction nor conditioned by a condition. His existence is necessary (ضروري) and His non-existence is impossible (ممتنع). Other than Him among the possible existents (الموجودات الممكنة) exists under an estimation, which is the estimation of the existence of its cause; it is the restricted existence that exists through another without necessity in its essence.

And if the reality (حقية) of a thing is its establishment, then He, the Exalted, is Real by His essence, and others only become real and realized through Him.

If you contemplate this meaning with true contemplation, you will find, firstly: that all things rely in their existence upon Him, the Exalted, and also rely in the system occurring in them generally, and in the particular systems occurring in every one of their species and every one of their individuals, upon Him, the Exalted.

Secondly: that the existential perfections (الكمالات الوجودية) which are attributes of existence—such as knowledge, power, life, hearing, sight, oneness, creation, ownership, self-sufficiency, praise, and expertise, among what was counted in the previous verses or was not counted—are attributes subsisting through Him, the Exalted, according to what befits the courtyard of His greatness and the might of His holiness. Because they are existential attributes and existence subsists through Him, the Exalted, they are either identical to His essence (عين ذاته), like knowledge and power, or attributes external to His essence abstracted from His act, like creation, provision, and mercy.

Thirdly: that the acceptance of a partner in His essence or in His governance, and everything that carries the meaning of lack and deficiency, is negated from Him, the Exalted. These are the negative attributes (الصفات السلبية), such as the negation of a partner, the negation of multiplicity, the negation of body, place, time, ignorance, incapacity, falsehood, and cessation, among others.

For the absoluteness of His existence and its lack of restriction by any restriction negates from Him every privative meaning; i.e., the affirmation of existence absolutely, for the return of the negation of negation is to affirmation.

Perhaps His saying: وَأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ "and because Allah is the All-exalted, the All-great" signifies the establishment of attributes for Him in both their stages, based on the name "the All-exalted" (الْعَلِيُ) signifying the meaning of His transcendence (تنزهه) above what does not befit His courtyard—so He is the gathering point of the negative attributes—and "the All-great" (الكبير) signifying His vastness for every existential perfection—so He is the gathering point of the affirmative attributes.

And the beginning of the verse is a proof for its end, and its end is a proof for His gathering, the Exalted, of both affirmative and negative attributes together, according to what has been previously established. Thus, He is the Essence gathering all attributes of perfection; so He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

And His saying: وَأَنَّ ما يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الْباطِلُ "and whatever they invoke besides Him is the False" follows the same course as His saying: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُ "That is because Allah is the Real." For those whom they invoke as gods have nothing of reality, nor do they have any part in creation or governance, because a partner in Divinity and Lordship is false (باطل), with no truth in it. And since it is false under every estimation, no creation or governance relies upon it at all.

The Real (الحق), the All-exalted (العلي), and the All-great (الكبير) are three of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى). It has been realized from what preceded that "the Real" is in the meaning of the Necessary Existent (واجب الوجود), and that "the All-exalted" is among the negative attributes, and "the All-great" is among the affirmative attributes, close in meaning to our saying: "the one gathering the attributes of perfection."

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ الْفُلْكَ تَجْرِي فِي الْبَحْرِ بِنِعْمَتِ اللهِ لِيُرِيَكُمْ مِنْ آياتِهِ "Have you not seen that the ships sail on the sea by Allah’s blessing, that He may show you some of His signs?" (31:31) etc. The preposition (الباء) in بِنِعْمَتِ اللهِ "by Allah's blessing" is for causality (للسببية), and the mention of blessing is like a preamble for the end of the verse, and in it is an allusion to the obligation of thanking Him for His blessing, because thanking the Benefactor is obligatory.

The meaning is: Have you not seen that the ships sail and move in the sea because of the blessing of Allah, which are the causes of their sailing, such as the wind, the moisture of the water, and other things.

Some have considered that the preposition (الباء) is for transitivity (للتعدية) or accompaniment (المعية), and what is meant by the blessing is what the ships carry of food and other provisions of life.

He concluded the verse with His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ "There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]." (31:31). The "very patient" (الصبار) and "very grateful" (الشكور)—i.e., one who is very patient during adversity and very grateful during prosperity—is a metonymy for the believer, according to what has been said.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا غَشِيَهُمْ مَوْجٌ كَالظُّلَلِ دَعَوُا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ "When waves cover them like awnings, they invoke Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him..." (31:32) etc. Al-Raghib said: The awning (الظلة) is a cloud that provides shade, and it is mostly said regarding what is deemed unhealthy and disliked. He said: كَأَنَّهُ ظُلَّةٌ "as if it were an awning" (7:171), عَذابُ يَوْمِ الظُّلَّةِ "the punishment of the day of the awning" (26:189). End quote.

The meaning is: when waves like pieces of clouds cover and surround them in the sea, they turn exclusively to Allah and invoke Him for salvation while being sincere to Him in religion (مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ); i.e., in that is a proof that their primordial nature (الفطرة) is upon monotheism (التوحيد).

And His saying: فَلَمَّا نَجَّاهُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ فَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ "But when He delivers them to the land, [only] some of them are moderate." (31:32). The moderate (المقتصد) is the one who follows the middle path (القصد); i.e., the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), and what is meant by it is the monotheism to which their primordial nature guided them at that time. In the expression using the partitive "some" (مِنْ), there is a belittling of their number; i.e., when Allah the Glorified saved these who were invoking with sincerity to the land, only a few of them were moderate.

And His saying: وَما يَجْحَدُ بِآياتِنا إِلَّا كُلُّ خَتَّارٍ كَفُورٍ "And no one impugns Our signs except every ungrateful traitor." (31:32). The traitor (الختار) is an intensive form of treachery (الختر), which is extreme perfidy. In the context is a proof for multiplicity, and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ "O mankind! Be wary of your Lord..." (31:33). When He had presented the healing and sufficient proofs and exhortations, He gathered them at their conclusion in a general address calling them to godwariness (التقوى) and warning them of the Day of Resurrection, in which no one will avail another except through faith (الإيمان) and godwariness (التقوى).

Al-Raghib said: Recompense (الجزاء) is sufficiency and adequacy. And he said: It is said: "I deceived (غررت) so-and-one," meaning I caught his heedlessness and obtained from him what I wanted. Heedlessness (الغرة) is a lapse in wakefulness, and deception (الغرار) is heedlessness with a slumber. Until he said: So the deceiver (الغرور) is everything that deceives man, such as wealth, status, desire, and Satan. It has been interpreted as Satan, as he is the most wicked of deceivers, and as the world, because it is said: "The world deceives, harms, and passes." End quote.

So the meaning of the verse is: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ "O mankind! Be wary of your Lord," and He is Allah the Glorified, وَاخْشَوْا يَوْماً "and fear a day," and it is the Day of Resurrection, لا يَجْزِي "which will not atone," will not avail, والِدٌ عَنْ وَلَدِهِ وَلا مَوْلُودٌ هُوَ جازٍ "a father for his child, nor shall a child atone," avail or suffice, عَنْ والِدِهِ "for his father," شَيْئاً "in any way." إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ "Indeed Allah’s promise" of resurrection حَقٌ "is true," established and will not be broken. فَلا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ الْحياةُ الدُّنْيا "So do not let the life of the world deceive you" with its deceptive adornment, وَلا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللهِ الْغَرُورُ "nor let the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah," i.e., the genus of what deceives man from the affairs of the worldly life, or specifically Satan.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ وَيُنَزِّلُ الْغَيْثَ وَيَعْلَمُ ما فِي الْأَرْحامِ وَما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ ما ذا تَكْسِبُ غَداً وَما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ بِأَيِّ أَرْضٍ تَمُوتُ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌ "Indeed the knowledge of the Hour is with Allah. He sends down the rain, and He knows what is in the wombs. No soul knows what it will earn tomorrow, and no soul knows in what land it will die. Indeed Allah is All-knowing, All-aware." (31:34). Rain (الغيث) is the downpour, and the meaning of the sentences of the verse is clear.

The Glorified has counted three matters to which His knowledge is attached: the knowledge of the Hour, which is among what Allah has reserved for Himself and none knows it but He—and the restriction is indicated by His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ "Indeed the knowledge of the Hour is with Allah"—and the sending down of rain and the knowledge of what is in the wombs, which are specific to Him, the Exalted, unless He teaches them to another.

He counted two other matters of which man is ignorant, and thereby he is ignorant of everything that will happen to him of events: His saying: وَما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ ما ذا تَكْسِبُ غَداً "No soul knows what it will earn tomorrow," and His saying: وَما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ بِأَيِّ أَرْضٍ تَمُوتُ "and no soul knows in what land it will die."

The intention was a reminder that Allah knows everything, minute and grand, even the likes of the Hour, the knowledge of which is not facilitated for creation, while you are ignorant of the most important things that concern you of knowledge. So Allah knows and you do not know; therefore, beware of associating partners with Him, rebelling against His command, and turning away from His call, lest you perish through your ignorance.

(Narrative Discourse)

In Kamal al-Din, with its chain of transmission to Hammad ibn Abi Ziyad, who said: I asked my master Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَأَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعَمَهُ ظاهِرَةً وَباطِنَةً "and He has showered upon you His blessings, the outward and the inward." He said: "The outward blessing is the manifest Imam, and the inward is the hidden Imam."

I say: This is of the type of application (الجري), and the verse is broader in its indication.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, with its chain of transmission from Jabir, who said: A man said in the presence of Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): وَأَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعَمَهُ ظاهِرَةً وَباطِنَةً "and He has showered upon you His blessings, the outward and the inward." He said: "As for the outward blessing, it is the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—and what he brought of the knowledge of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and His oneness—and as for the inward blessing, it is our guardianship (ولايتنا), the People of the House, and the bond of our love." The narration continues.

I say: It is like the one before it.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ "and He has showered upon you..." the verse. And in the narration of al-Dahhak from Ibn Abbas, who said: I asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about it, and he said: "O Ibn Abbas, as for what is outward, it is Islam—and what is besides Allah of your creation and what He has poured upon you of provision—and as for what is inward, it is the veiling of your evil deeds and His not exposing you for them. O Ibn Abbas, indeed Allah the Exalted says: 'Three things I have granted to the believer which he did not have: the prayer of the believers for him after the cessation of his deeds, and I made for him a third of his wealth to expiate his sins, and the third is that I veiled his evil deeds and did not expose him for any of them—for if I had manifested them against him, his family and those besides them would have rejected him.'"

I say: Something close to it was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through various paths from Ibn Abbas, and the hadith is like the ones before it in being an application (الجري).

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission from Umar ibn Udhaynah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): In a hadith: And the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Every newborn is born upon the primordial nature (الفطرة)—meaning upon the knowledge that Allah is his Creator—and that is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ 'If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ الْفُلْكَ تَجْرِي فِي الْبَحْرِ بِنِعْمَتِ اللهِ "Have you not seen that the ships sail on the sea by Allah’s blessing." He said: "The ships sail in the sea by the power of Allah."

And in it, regarding His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ "There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]." He said: "The one who is patient during poverty and need—and thanks Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, in all his states."

In al-Majma', regarding the verse, and in the hadith: "Faith is two halves: a half is patience and a half is gratitude."

I say: This is taken from the verse, for it has passed that it is a metonymy for the believer.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا كُلُّ خَتَّارٍ كَفُورٍ "except every ungrateful traitor." He said: "The traitor (الختار) is the deceiver." And regarding His saying: إِنَّ وَعدَ اللهِ حَقٌ "Indeed Allah’s promise is true." He said: "That is the Resurrection."

In the Irshad of al-Mufid, from the speech of the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) to a man—whom he heard disparaging the world without knowledge of what he ought to say regarding its meaning: "The world is an abode of truth for him who is truthful with it, an abode of well-being for him who understands it, and an abode of wealth for him who takes provision from it. It is the mosque of Allah's prophets, the place of descent for His revelation, the place of prayer for His angels, and the marketplace for His friends; they earned mercy in it and profited Paradise in it. So who is it that disparages it? It has announced its departure, called out its separation, and announced its own death. Thus, through its joy, it created longing for joy, and through its trial, it warned of trial, as a way of frightening, warning, encouraging, and terrifying.

O you who disparage the world and are deceived by its deception, when did it deceive you? Was it by the falling places of your fathers in decay, or by the falling places of your mothers under the soil? How much did you nurse with your two hands and tend to the sick with your two hands, seeking for them healing—and you sought descriptions from doctors for them, and sought medicine for them. You did not benefit them by your seeking, nor did you intercede for them by your intercession. The world represented to you through them your own falling place and your own bed—where your weeping will not benefit you, nor will your loved ones avail you."

In al-Khisal, from Abu Usamah from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: He said: "Shall I not inform you of five things that Allah has not disclosed to anyone of His creation?" He said: I said: Yes. He said: إِنَّ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ وَيُنَزِّلُ الْغَيْثَ ـ وَيَعْلَمُ ما فِي الْأَرْحامِ وَما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ ما ذا تَكْسِبُ غَداً ـ وَما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ بِأَيِّ أَرْضٍ تَمُوتُ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌ "Indeed the knowledge of the Hour is with Allah. He sends down the rain, and He knows what is in the wombs. No soul knows what it will earn tomorrow, and no soul knows in what land it will die. Indeed Allah is All-knowing, All-aware."

I say: There are very many narrations from the Prophet and the Imams (peace be upon them) informing about their future states and about the time and place of their deaths, and they qualify this narration and those in its meaning by divine teaching; however, some narrations refuse qualification and pay no heed to their matter.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ikrimah: That a man called al-Warath—from the Banu Mazin ibn Hafsah ibn Qays Ghaylan—came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: "O Muhammad, when will the Hour occur? Our lands have become barren, so when will they become fertile? I have left my wife pregnant, so when will she give birth? I have known what I earned today, so what will I earn tomorrow? I have known in which land I was born, so in which land will I die?" Then this verse was revealed.

I say: The hadith is not without issue due to the lack of correspondence of the verse to the segments of the question.

In it, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "Nothing was obscured from your Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله except five of the secrets of the Unseen (الغيب)—this verse at the end of Luqman until the end of the Surah."
